4-1.v: What Is a Cost? Start Here.

Before any formula or classification, understand one thing: a cost is simply the money a business sacrifices to get something done — buy materials, pay workers, run a machine, rent a building. Every business activity has a cost attached to it. The FMAA exam tests whether you understand how those costs behave — meaning, what happens to them when the business does more or less.
Topic 1: Variable Costs — Costs That Follow Activity

Imagine you own a small juice bar. Every cup of juice needs one orange. If you make 100 cups, you need 100 oranges. If you make 500 cups, you need 500 oranges. The orange cost follows your production exactly — it goes up when you produce more, and down when you produce less. That is a variable cost.
Total Variable Cost = Cost per Unit × Number of Units Produced
The critical insight for the exam: the TOTAL variable cost changes with activity, but the cost PER UNIT stays constant. One orange always costs the same per cup — whether you make 10 cups or 10,000.

Direct material cost = $3 per unit.
Produce 1,000 units → Total cost = $3,000
Produce 4,000 units → Total cost = $12,000
Cost per unit = $3 in both cases. ✓ Variable.
Topic 2: Fixed Costs — Costs That Stay Regardless

Now think about the rent for your juice bar. You pay $2,000/month whether you make 10 cups or 10,000 cups. The landlord does not care how busy you are. That $2,000 is a fixed cost — it does not change with how much you produce. It is committed the moment you sign the lease.
Total Fixed Cost = Constant (does not change with activity)

Here is where beginners get confused — and where the exam loves to trick you: the total fixed cost stays the same, but the fixed cost per unit goes down as you produce more. If rent is $2,000 and you make 1,000 cups, rent per cup = $2.00. Make 4,000 cups and rent per cup = $0.50. The total did not move — but the per-unit amount dropped.

Exam trap: 'Total fixed cost stays the same' does NOT mean 'fixed cost per unit stays the same.' Total fixed cost is constant. Fixed cost per unit falls as production rises, and rises as production falls. This distinction appears in almost every FMAA exam on this topic.

Factory rent = $50,000/month.
Produce 1,000 units → Rent per unit = $50.00
Produce 5,000 units → Rent per unit = $10.00
Total rent = $50,000 in both cases. ✓ Fixed.
Topic 3: Mixed Costs — Part Fixed, Part Variable

Some costs do not fit neatly into either category. Your electricity bill at the juice bar has two parts: a fixed monthly connection fee ($100 no matter what), plus a charge per kWh you actually use. The more you produce, the more electricity you use — but you always pay that base $100. This is a mixed cost, also called a semi-variable cost.
Total Mixed Cost = Fixed Component + (Variable Rate × Activity Level)

Electricity: $100 fixed + $0.08 per kWh used.
Use 2,000 kWh → Total = $100 + $160 = $260
Use 5,000 kWh → Total = $100 + $400 = $500
The $100 never disappears — it is the fixed floor.
The Big Picture: All Three Side by Side
The Relevant Range — One Important Condition

Fixed costs are only truly fixed within a relevant range — the normal range of activity the business expects to operate in. If production doubles beyond that range, you may need a bigger factory and rent jumps. The exam assumes you are within the relevant range unless told otherwise.

Exam shortcut: When a question says 'assume costs behave in a linear fashion' or gives you a specific activity range, it is telling you to treat fixed costs as fixed and variable costs as constant per unit within that range. Do not overthink it.
Why This Topic Matters for the Whole Section

Everything in Cost Management — CVP analysis, budgeting, variance analysis, pricing decisions — is built on your ability to separate fixed from variable costs. If you are not clear on this distinction, every topic that follows will feel harder than it needs to be. Master this unit and the rest of Section D will make much more sense.

v: Variable cost: total changes, per-unit is constant
Fixed cost: total is constant, per-unit changes
Mixed cost: has both a fixed floor and a variable component
Relevant range: the activity range within which these rules hold
This classification is the foundation of CVP, budgeting, and pricing

4-2.v: Manufacturing overhead (MOH) = all production costs that are NOT direct materials or direct labor. It cannot be traced to a specific unit, so it must be allocated. Accurate overhead allocation is critical for pricing, profitability analysis, and GAAP compliance.

r: Lubricants, cleaning supplies, small tools not traceable to specific products
Plant superintendent, janitorial, quality control salaries

4-2.v: Budgeted Manufacturing Overhead ÷ Budgeted Activity Level of Allocation Base

4-2.l: Calculating and Using the Predetermined Rate
4-2.v:
Budgeted annual OH: $480,000 | Budgeted direct labor hours: 40,000
Predetermined rate: $480,000 ÷ 40,000 = $12 per DLH

In March, Job #205 uses 150 DLH:
OH applied to Job #205 = 150 × $12 = $1,800

Reverse formula (often tested):
If predetermined rate = $12/DLH and budgeted DLH = 40,000:
Budgeted OH = $12 × 40,000 = $480,000

4-2.l: Why Departmental Rates Are More Accurate
4-2.v:
Company has 2 departments:
  Dept A: automated, machine-intensive → uses machine hours as base
  Dept B: labor-intensive → uses direct labor hours as base

Using ONE plant-wide rate based on DLH:
  Product X spends 80% of time in Dept A (machines) and 20% in Dept B (labor)
  → Product X is UNDER-costed (assigned less OH than it actually causes)
  → Products that use more machines are subsidized by labor-intensive products

Using DEPARTMENTAL rates:
  Dept A rate = $40/machine-hour | Dept B rate = $15/DLH
  Product X is accurately charged for its heavy machine usage in Dept A

ABC assigns overhead to products based on their consumption of activities. Each activity has its own cost pool and its own cost driver — the measure of what causes that cost to occur. ABC is more accurate than traditional methods but more complex.

Traditional: Pool all OH ($500,000) ÷ Total DLH (50,000) = $10/DLH

ABC identifies 3 activity pools:
  Machine setup: $150,000 ÷ 500 setups = $300/setup
  Quality inspection: $200,000 ÷ 1,000 inspections = $200/inspection
  Machine running: $150,000 ÷ 30,000 machine-hours = $5/MH

Product A (10 setups, 20 inspections, 5,000 MH, 10,000 DLH):
  Traditional: 10,000 DLH × $10 = $100,000
  ABC: (10×$300)+(20×$200)+(5,000×$5) = $3,000+$4,000+$25,000 = $32,000

Product A is dramatically OVER-costed under traditional — ABC reveals its true cost.

ABC key principle: Activities consume resources (costs); products consume activities. A cost driver is what CAUSES the activity cost to be incurred — machine hours cause machine costs; setups cause setup costs. Products that require fewer setups should not subsidize those that require many.

4-3.v: Variance analysis compares actual results to what was planned. It enables management by exception — focus only on significant variances that need investigation, not on every line item.

r: Price and efficiency differences at actual volume
The pure volume effect (sold more or fewer units than planned

4-3.v:
Actual: sold 20,000 units, revenue $2,500,000, variable costs $1,900,200, fixed $570,000, OI $29,800
Flexible budget (at 20,000 units): revenue $2,400,000, variable $1,760,000, fixed $552,000, OI $88,000
Static budget (at 24,000 units): revenue $2,880,000, variable $2,112,000, fixed $552,000, OI $216,000

Flexible budget variance: $29,800 − $88,000 = −$58,200 Unfavorable (price/efficiency issues)
Sales volume variance: $88,000 − $216,000 = −$128,000 Unfavorable (sold 4,000 fewer units)
Static budget variance: $29,800 − $216,000 = −$186,200 Unfavorable (total underperformance)
(Actual Quantity − Standard Quantity Allowed) × Standard Price = (AQ − SQ) × SP
(Actual Price − Standard Price) × Actual Quantity = (AP − SP) × AQ

4-3.l: Medina Co. Football Leather — Full DM Variance Analysis
4-3.v:
Standard: 1 sq meter leather per football @ $5.00/meter
Actual: Produced 250 footballs; used 290 meters @ $4.50/meter

Actual cost: 290 × $4.50 = $1,305
Standard cost allowed: 250 × 1 × $5.00 = $1,250
Total DM Variance: $1,305 − $1,250 = $55 Unfavorable

DM Quantity Variance: (290 − 250) × $5.00 = 40 × $5.00 = $200 Unfavorable
(Used 40 more meters than allowed — may indicate low-quality material causing waste)

DM Price Variance: ($4.50 − $5.00) × 290 = −$0.50 × 290 = $(145) Favorable
(Paid $0.50 less per meter than standard — may be lower quality material)

Check: $200 U + $(145) F = $55 U ✓
Interpretation: Bought cheaper (inferior?) leather — saved $145 on price but wasted $200 more in usage — net unfavorable.
(Actual Rate − Standard Rate) × Actual Hours = (AR − SR) × AH
(Actual Hours − Standard Hours Allowed) × Standard Rate = (AH − SH) × SR

Standard: 0.5 hours per unit @ $18/hour
Actual: Produced 500 units; worked 270 hours @ $19/hour

Standard hours allowed: 500 × 0.5 = 250 hours

DL Rate Variance: ($19 − $18) × 270 = $1 × 270 = $270 Unfavorable
(Paid $1 more per hour than standard — may reflect wage increases or use of higher-skill workers)

DL Efficiency Variance: (270 − 250) × $18 = 20 × $18 = $360 Unfavorable
(Used 20 more hours than standard allowed — workers were less efficient than planned)

Total DL Variance: $270 + $360 = $630 Unfavorable
Actual Fixed OH − Budgeted Fixed OH
Budgeted Fixed OH − Fixed OH Applied (Standard Rate × Standard Input for Actual Output)

Budgeted fixed OH: $60,000 | Standard fixed OH rate: $6/DLH (based on 10,000 DLH budget)
Actual fixed OH incurred: $63,000
Actual production: 9,000 units | Standard DLH per unit: 1 hour
Standard DLH allowed for actual output: 9,000 × 1 = 9,000 hours
Fixed OH applied: 9,000 × $6 = $54,000

Fixed OH Spending Variance: $63,000 − $60,000 = $3,000 Unfavorable
(Actual fixed costs were $3,000 more than budgeted — e.g. rent increase)

Fixed OH Production-Volume Variance: $60,000 − $54,000 = $6,000 Unfavorable
(Produced 9,000 units instead of 10,000 — underutilized capacity; $6,000 of fixed OH unabsorbed)

Total Fixed OH Variance: $63,000 − $54,000 = $9,000 Unfavorable = $3,000 + $6,000 ✓

Fixed OH volume variance = CAPACITY UTILIZATION measure. Positive (unfavorable) = underproduced vs budget. Negative (favorable) = overproduced vs budget. It has NO connection to actual costs incurred — purely a measure of whether the factory was used as planned.

4-4.v: Performance Measurement — ROI and Residual Income

The 80-20 rule: 80% of profits come from 20% of customers; 20% of customers are often completely unprofitable. Customer and product profitability analysis identifies which customers and products to grow, reprice, or eliminate.
Operating Income of Business Unit ÷ Assets of Business Unit

ROI measures the percentage return earned on invested assets. A manager will accept a new project only if it raises (or at least maintains) the division's ROI. This can lead to rejecting profitable projects — the main weakness of ROI.

Division current ROI: $600,000 income ÷ $3,000,000 assets = 20%
Required rate of return: 12%
New project: $80,000 income on $500,000 assets = 16% ROI

Division manager's view: New project ROI (16%) < current ROI (20%)
→ Manager REJECTS the project (it dilutes the division's ROI)

Company's view: 16% project ROI > 12% required rate of return
→ Company WANTS the project (it creates value above cost of capital)

This conflict is ROI's key limitation — it discourages managers from accepting value-creating projects that are below their current ROI.
Operating Income − (Assets × Required Rate of Return)

New project contribution to RI:
  RI = $80,000 − ($500,000 × 12%) = $80,000 − $60,000 = $20,000 Positive

Since RI is positive, the project ADDS value above the required return
→ Manager using RI would ACCEPT the project ✓

Medina Division example: Assets $4,000,000, Operating Income $600,000, Required Rate 10%
RI = $600,000 − ($4,000,000 × 10%) = $600,000 − $400,000 = $200,000
(Earned $200,000 more than the minimum required return)

r: Easy to compare segments of different sizes
Correctly evaluates all projects earning above required rate
Managers reject value-creating projects below current ROI
Cannot compare divisions of different sizes directly
Cost variances vs budget (e.g. manufacturing dept)
ROI or Residual Income (e.g. autonomous subsidiary)

4-5.v: CVP (breakeven) analysis examines the relationship between sales volume, costs, and profit. It is the primary tool for short-run decisions about pricing, product mix, and profitability planning.

v: All costs are purely fixed or variable (no mixed costs in the model)
Total revenues and costs are linear within the relevant range
Unit selling price is constant — no volume discounts
Unit variable costs are constant — economies of scale ignored
Sales mix is constant (for multi-product companies)
Time value of money is ignored

4-5.v: Selling Price per Unit − Variable Cost per Unit
Unit Contribution Margin ÷ Selling Price (expressed as %)
Total Fixed Costs ÷ Unit Contribution Margin
Total Fixed Costs ÷ Contribution Margin Ratio
(Fixed Costs + Target Profit) ÷ Unit Contribution Margin
A cost (or revenue) is relevant to a decision if and only if it meets BOTH conditions: (1) it occurs in the FUTURE, and (2) it DIFFERS between the alternatives being compared.

r: Past cost — cannot be changed regardless of decision
Future cost, same in all alternatives
Doesn't differ — won't affect the choice
Will affect the decision — include it
Future foregone benefit — always relevant when resources are constrained
Will disappear if a particular option is chosen

4-5.v:
Currently making Part X in-house. Outside supplier offers $18/unit.
In-house costs per unit: Direct materials $6 + Direct labor $5 + Variable OH $3 + Fixed OH $5 = $19

Should the company buy outside?

Relevant comparison:
  Buy: $18/unit (avoidable variable cost)
  Make: $14/unit (variable costs only — $6 + $5 + $3)
  Fixed OH: $5/unit is UNAVOIDABLE — it continues either way → NOT relevant

Decision: Make at $14 < Buy at $18 → MAKE in-house ✓

BUT if $3 of fixed OH is avoidable (department can be shut down):
  Make relevant cost = $14 + $3 = $17 < Buy $18 → Still MAKE
  Make relevant cost = $14 + $5 = $19 > Buy $18 → BUY outside

Sunk costs are the most common exam trap in relevant cost questions. No matter how large a past investment was, it is completely irrelevant to a current or future decision. 'We already spent $500,000 on this equipment' — irrelevant. Only future, differential costs matter.
